title: Let them off. Then get on.
norm: Stand to the side of the door and let people get off the bus or train before you board.
wit: Two crowds trying to pass through one door is just physics, losing.
affects: elders and children in the crush
goesRight: Off first, on second, and the doorway clears in seconds - the train waits less and nobody gets crushed.

impact.cause: Boarding while others are still trying to get off
impact.mechanism: Two opposing crowds jam the doorway, so the doors stay open longer at every stop and the crush-risk rises
impact.effect: Slower journeys for everyone and a real fall-and-injury risk for elders and children
impact.publisher: Transit operations research (e.g. Transportation Research Board)
impact.supported_by: Simultaneous boarding and alighting increases door dwell time and passenger conflict at the doorway.
impact.caveat: Well-established operations principle; we show the mechanism rather than an Indian-network number.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: The priority seat is a promise.
norm: Keep the reserved seat free for those who need it - elders, pregnant women, and people with disabilities - and give yours up if asked.
wit: It costs you a two-minute stand. It saves someone a fall.
goesRight: A free priority seat lets the people who most need to sit travel safely, with dignity, and without asking.

impact.cause: Occupying a reserved seat when you don't need it
impact.mechanism: People with limited mobility must stand and are thrown off balance by braking and jolts in a moving vehicle
impact.effect: Avoidable falls and injuries, and the everyday indignity of being left standing
impact.source_title: Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 - accessible transport and reserved seating
impact.supported_by: Indian law provides for reserved seating and accessible public transport for persons with disabilities.
impact.caveat: Framed as the reason for the norm, not as a measured injury statistic.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.
